briefing.opening: Good day. We will now begin the climate change expert spokesperson briefing for May 23, 2026. Today's climate-related news will encompass the reality of global climate threats, domestic and international carbon neutrality efforts, and the challenges faced in that process.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Climate Threats: Extreme Weather and Ecosystem Emergency
sections.content: Today, extreme weather phenomena and their severe impacts have been reported across various parts of the world. The UK recorded its highest temperature of the year, 30.5°C, in the Kent region, marking the first time temperatures have exceeded 30°C in May since 2012, with forecasters warning of more intense heatwaves [26]. France also experienced heatwaves, indicating that temperature fluctuations due to climate change are becoming more common [15]. Furthermore, a complex outlook emerged for the Central Atlantic region, suggesting a potential decrease in the number of hurricanes this year, but with climate change possibly making hurricanes more severe [16]. These extreme climatic conditions are threatening global plant species by shrinking their habitats [12] and are impacting rice crops, raising concerns about food security [20]. Notably, climate change is disproportionately burdening vulnerable populations, such as disrupting the childhoods of at-risk children [24].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Technological Innovation
sections.content: The Republic of Korea continues its diverse efforts and technological innovations to achieve carbon neutrality goals. Citizens are encouraged to actively participate in carbon neutrality practices, such as using bicycles and tumblers, and can receive benefits of up to 100,000 KRW [1, 2]. In academia, Professor Hyung-Mo Jeong's team at Sungkyunkwan University has achieved a technological breakthrough by developing a groundbreaking water-splitting catalyst original technology that produces eco-friendly green hydrogen using 'hidden oxygen' [3]. Furthermore, Jeongeup City is fully committed to realizing a sustainable carbon-neutral and climate-safe city by 2026 [4]. Corporate contributions are also active, with Korea South-East Power (KOEN) accelerating AI-based smart work and successfully hosting the '2026 Carbon Neutral Biochar Forum' [5]. The Icheon City Volunteer Center is also leading ESG practices through plogging with colleagues [6], demonstrating a widespread positive movement towards carbon neutrality across society.

sections.title: ⚠️ Reality and Challenges of Domestic Energy Transition
sections.content: Despite positive efforts, the domestic energy transition still faces significant challenges. The fact that industrial cities' renewable energy self-sufficiency rate is merely 3.2% raises a serious alarm about the pace of energy transition [8]. By 2025, discarded solar panels are expected to reach 2,500 tons, making their recovery and advanced recycling an urgent issue [9]. Furthermore, while the 'used battery' market is becoming active, it faces numerous challenges such as dual regulations and a shortage of raw materials, anticipating difficulties in establishing a circular economy [10]. Moreover, the energy pledges of the ruling and opposition parties regarding nuclear power and renewable energy are 'poles apart' [7], indicating the need for fundamental discussions on the consistency and direction of national energy policy.

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Action: Hopes and Complex Discussions
sections.content: The international community continues to engage in complex discussions alongside hopeful news regarding climate change response. Notably, the news that the worst-case climate change scenario has been officially canceled can be interpreted as a result of global efforts and a positive signal [11]. While an argument is being made that the core of climate action lies in building the power of the working class [14], Pacific Island students and farmers are playing a crucial role in fighting against climate change [17, 21]. Meanwhile, regarding the approach to climate change responsibility, conflicting views coexist: news that a Senate amendment will allow active climate change litigation [18], and the opposing view that addressing it through Congress rather than the courts is most effective [19]. These diverse perspectives demonstrate that responding to climate change is a complex challenge that extends beyond scientific issues to encompass social, economic, and political dimensions.

briefing.closing: This concludes the climate briefing for May 23, 2026. We hope that today's briefing has provided a clear understanding of the climate threats facing the world, alongside hopeful progress and the challenges that still need to be addressed. We look forward to seeing you at the next briefing.
